title: Bonfire toffee
subtitle: Dark, brittle treacle toffee cooked to hard crack
excerpt: A very dark, brittle toffee made from black treacle, golden syrup, dark brown sugar, and butter cooked to hard-crack stage (around 150°C). The black treacle gives it a bitter, almost smoky depth that distinguishes bonfire toffee from golden butterscotch. It sets to a hard, brittle slab that is broken into shards rather than cut. Traditionally made for Bonfire Night in the north of England.
sourceNotes: Bonfire toffee (also called Plot toffee or treacle toffee) is a regional British confection traditionally associated with Bonfire Night (5 November, Guy Fawkes Night) and particularly with the north of England and Yorkshire. Recipes appear in northern English household cookbooks from the late 19th century. It differs from golden toffee in the use of black treacle rather than golden syrup as the primary sweetener, giving a very dark colour and a bitter depth. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), includes toffee recipes with treacle. The recipe here is cooked to hard-crack stage so the finished toffee is brittle rather than chewy.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bonfire toffee keeps in an airtight tin for up to 2 weeks at room temperature. Separate pieces with baking paper to prevent sticking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: target sugar temperature. Hard-crack stage: 149 to 154°C. At this temperature a drop in cold water forms rigid, brittle threads. Below 140°C the toffee will be chewy; above 160°C it will taste burnt.

recipeTools.notes: lightly oiled 20 × 15 cm tin or similar

glossaryTerms.definition: The sugar cooking stage reached at 149 to 154°C at which point the sugar has lost almost all its water. A drop of the syrup in cold water forms rigid, brittle threads that break cleanly. This is the temperature required for hard, brittle toffees, butterscotch, and lollipops. Below this temperature (at soft-crack stage, 132 to 143°C) toffee will be chewy rather than brittle. A sugar thermometer is required: the difference between a chewy toffee and a brittle one is only a few degrees.

content.text: and sets completely rigid. A sugar thermometer is not optional here: the difference between chewy toffee and brittle toffee is a few degrees. The black treacle burns easily; do not walk away from the pan once it is boiling. Pour into the tin the moment it hits temperature. The mixture is around 150°C when poured. It is extremely dangerous. Keep children well away from the pan.

Lightly oil a shallow tin about 20 × 15 cm. Put all the ingredients in a heavy-based saucepan over low heat. Stir gently until the sugar has dissolved and the butter has melted. Stop stirring. Increase the heat to medium-high and bring to a steady boil. Cook, without stirring, until the mixture reaches 150°C on a sugar thermometer. Watch carefully: the black treacle makes the mixture very dark and it is easy to miss the colour change indicating burning.

The moment the thermometer reads 150°C, remove from the heat immediately and pour into the prepared tin. Tilt to level. Leave to cool and set completely, about 1 hour. When fully set, cover the tin with a clean tea towel and strike with a rolling pin to break the toffee into irregular shards. Store in an airtight tin between layers of baking paper.
